properties.name: Mewar Samant System — Umrao (Salumber Head)
properties.polityUnit: Umrao — 16 first-category + 32 second-category + Gol ke Umrao (hundreds)

facts: Mewar's Umrao (samant) system was three-tiered — the Salumber Thakur headed the 16 first-category Umraos and governed Udaipur in the Maharana's absence.
Alone in Rajputana, Mewar refused the Mughal Mansabdari succession-tax — the state's samants kept their pre-Akbar prerogatives.
The Salumber Thakur's troops joined Auwa's Kushal Singh in the 1857 rebellion, the last time the Umrao system flexed independent military power.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 12 Ch 2 (Medieval Administrative System of Rajasthan)

properties.name: Marwar Feudal Categories — Rajvi/Sardar/Ganayat/Mutsaddi
properties.polityUnit: Four samant categories with distinct rights

facts: Marwar samants were classified as Rajvi (close royal kin up to 3 generations), Sardar, Ganayat and Mutsaddi.
Rajvi were exempt from Rekh (revenue), Hukmnama tax and Chakri (military service) — the highest hereditary tier in Rajputana.
The 1835 Jodhpur Legion (raised by the British) uprooted the pre-Mughal Marwar military balance; the samants' revolt at Erinpura in 1857 was partly a defence of that balance.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 12 Ch 2 (Medieval Administrative System of Rajasthan)

properties.name: Jaipur Kotri System (12 Kotris of Prithvi Singh)
properties.polityUnit: 12 permanent Jagirs named after the 12 sons of Prithvi Singh

facts: Jaipur's samant categories were fixed by Maharaja Prithvi Singh — 12 permanent Jagirs held in the names of his 12 sons, called Kotri.
The Musahib was the Pradhan (prime minister) of Jaipur — a title unique to this state.
The Kachwaha-Mughal alliance from Bhagwant Das (1568) onward made Jaipur the most-Mughal-adjacent Rajputana court; Man Singh I's Kotri system was the model for it.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 12 Ch 2 (Medieval Administrative System of Rajasthan)

properties.name: Kota Feudal System — 30 Rajvi Sardars (Hada Chauhans)

facts: Kota samants were called Rajvi; 30 Rajvi Sardars ran the state, most from the Hada Chauhan branch.
Only two Rajputana courts (Kota, Bundi) called their Pradhan a "Diwan" — a Rajput adaptation of the Mughal revenue-administration title.
The rebel Kota administration of Oct 1857-Mar 1858 (Jayadayal, Mehrab Khan) briefly bypassed the entire Rajvi hierarchy — the last time public + army defeated a Rajputana court from within.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 12 Ch 2 (Medieval Administrative System of Rajasthan)

properties.name: Bikaner Three-Tier Samant Categories
properties.polityUnit: 3 samant tiers based on lineage proximity

facts: Bikaner samants: (i) Rao Bika's direct male-line kin, (ii) other blood-related Rathores, (iii) pre-Rathore ruling houses (Bhati, Sankhla + others).
The Pradhan (prime minister) here carried the title Mukhtyar — again unique among Rajputana states.
Ganga Singh (1887-1943) modernised the samant system by anchoring it to a written Rekh register — often called the last active Rajputana samant reform.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 12 Ch 2 (Medieval Administrative System of Rajasthan)

properties.name: Jaisalmer Samant System — Davi / Jeevani (Left / Right)

facts: Jaisalmer's Bhati samants were divided into Davi (Left) and Jeevani (Right) categories from Rawal Harraj's reign onwards.
Jaisalmer was the ONLY Rajputana state that never collected a Succession Tax (Peshkashi / Nazrana / Hukmnama) from its samants.
The two-tier system reflected the Bhati court's ceremonial seating on left and right of the ruler — an unusually formal Rajput courtly arrangement.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 12 Ch 2 (Medieval Administrative System of Rajasthan)

properties.name: Bharatpur — Suraj Mal's Jat Administrative Reform
properties.polityUnit: Caste-blind administrative posts under a Mukhtyar Pradhan

facts: Raja Suraj Mal (r. 1756-1763) built a non-caste administrative structure — Bharatpur's Pradhan (called Mukhtyar) could be from any community.
His agricultural policy pushed the state's revenue to ₹17.5 lakh per year by 1763 (the final year of his reign) — the highest in any Rajputana Jat state.
A Kirti Stambh at Kumher records Suraj Mal's administrative rules — cited by Colonel Todd as the "most rational" Rajputana revenue code.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 12 Ch 2 (Medieval Administrative System of Rajasthan)

properties.name: Succession Tax Vocabulary Across Courts
properties.polityUnit: Different names of the same tax across courts

facts: Same tax, different names: Jodhpur — Peshkashi (later Hukmnama); Mewar & Jaipur — Nazrana; other states — Qaid Khalsa / Talwar Bandhai.
Jaisalmer alone was exempt — the Bhati court never took a succession tax.
The tax was in effect the renewal of the Jagir lease (Patta); non-payment triggered the Diwan to declare the Jagir Khalsa (crown-resumed).

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 12 Ch 2 (Medieval Administrative System of Rajasthan)

properties.name: Khalisa vs Jagir Land — the Four Jagir Types

facts: Khalisa land — direct crown estate; Hakim officers ran justice and revenue here.

Four Jagir types: (i) Samant Jagir — hereditary clan grant; (ii) Hukumat Jagir — salary grant to Mutsaddi, resumed on death; (iii) Bhom Jagir — border-defence grant, un-evictable; (iv) Sasan Jagir — tax-free (Maafi) grant to Charans, Bhats and temples.
Only Samant Jagir was hereditary — the other three reverted to Khalisa on the holder's death.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 12 Ch 2 (Medieval Administrative System of Rajasthan)

properties.name: Rent Collection Methods — Lata/Batai, Kunta, Mukata, Dori, Ghughri

facts: Lata/Batai — physical grain division at the threshing floor under an officer's supervision.
Kunta — estimation of the standing crop by eye, without weighing.
Mukata (lump sum), Dori (measured area) and Ghughri (equal to the seed given by the state) were three cash-and-kind variants used in leaner years.
Barani land (rain-fed) and Unnao land (irrigated by tank/well/baori) were assessed at different rates in every state.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 12 Ch 2 (Medieval Administrative System of Rajasthan)

properties.name: Bapidar vs Gair-Bapidar (Khudkashtkar vs Shikmi Kashtkar)

facts: Bapidar (a.k.a. Khudkashtkar) — hereditary land-owner peasant; could not be evicted, retained the field even after leaving in famine years.
Gair-Bapidar (a.k.a. Shikmi Kashtkar) — non-hereditary tenant farmer, closer to an agricultural labourer.
The saying Colonel Todd recorded — "Bhog ra dhani Raj ho, Bhom ra dhani ma chhau" — captures the ruler-peasant split: king owns the revenue, tiller owns the land.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 12 Ch 2 (Medieval Administrative System of Rajasthan)

properties.name: Bhomia & Grasia Samants — Border-Defence Grants

facts: Bhomia — jagir grantees who died defending the border or village; the grant was un-evictable in perpetuity.
Grasia — took a share of the harvest (gras) in return for military service; lax service could cost them the grant.
These were the two samant classes Todd said kept Rajputana border villages defensible against Mughal, Maratha and Pindari incursions.

properties.source: Rajasthan Adhyan Class 12 Ch 2 (Medieval Administrative System of Rajasthan)
